item.title: Dynamical scarring from scrambling in two-dimensional topological materials
item.description:
Author(s): Dominik Szpara, Szczepan Głodzik, and Nicholas Sedlmayr

Out-of-time ordered correlators are a probe of how the information of an initial perturbation is effectively scrambled under unitary time evolution, widely used to study quantum chaos. They have also been used to demonstrate that information is trapped in the zero-dimensional edge modes of topologic…


[Phys. Rev. B 113, 094303] Published Fri Mar 06, 2026

item.title: Magnetic excitations in the Kitaev material ${\mathrm{Na}}_{2}{\mathrm{IrO}}_{3}$ studied by neutron scattering
item.description:
Author(s): Alexandre Bertin, Hengdi Zhao, Gang Cao, Andrea Piovano, Paul Steffens, Alexandre Ivanov, and Markus Braden

Na2IrO3 is one of the most studied Kitaev materials, but the microscopic interactions remain controversial leaving the imminent question open, whether Na2IrO3 shows or approaches a quantum spin liquid phase. The authors overcome here the challenges to perform neutron scattering experiments on highly absorbing Na2IrO3 by aligning a large number of thin crystals. These experiments reveal a small gap in the magnetic excitations similar to the sister compound α-RuCl3. However, Na2IrO3 does not exhibit low-energy ferromagnetic excitations reflecting nearest-neighbor Kitaev and Heisenberg interaction terms with opposite signs.


[Phys. Rev. B 113, 094407] Published Wed Mar 04, 2026
item.content:encoded:
Author(s): Alexandre Bertin, Hengdi Zhao, Gang Cao, Andrea Piovano, Paul Steffens, Alexandre Ivanov, and Markus Braden

Na2IrO3 is one of the most studied Kitaev materials, but the microscopic interactions remain controversial leaving the imminent question open, whether Na2IrO3 shows or approaches a quantum spin liquid phase. The authors overcome here the challenges to perform neutron scattering experiments on highly absorbing Na2IrO3 by aligning a large number of thin crystals. These experiments reveal a small gap in the magnetic excitations similar to the sister compound α-RuCl3. However, Na2IrO3 does not exhibit low-energy ferromagnetic excitations reflecting nearest-neighbor Kitaev and Heisenberg interaction terms with opposite signs.


[Phys. Rev. B 113, 094407] Published Wed Mar 04, 2026

item.title: Microwave-induced transitions between weak localization and weak antilocalization in graphene
item.description:
Author(s): Jorge Navarro-Giraldo, Vinicius T. Santana, Oleksii Laguta, Dominik Bloos, Anastasia Bauernfeind, Marc Scheffler, Valentyn Laguta, D. Kurt Gaskill, and Petr Neugebauer

Weak localization and antilocalization are characteristic signatures of coherent electronic transport in 2D systems. Here, employing contactless microwave magnetoconductivity experiments with frequencies much larger than the electron decoherence rate, the authors report transitions between weak localization and weak antilocalization in graphene induced by the microwave frequency. These transitions fall outside the predictions of established models, suggesting the existence of novel mechanisms affecting electron coherence at high microwave frequencies.


[Phys. Rev. B 113, 125408] Published Wed Mar 04, 2026
item.content:encoded:
Author(s): Jorge Navarro-Giraldo, Vinicius T. Santana, Oleksii Laguta, Dominik Bloos, Anastasia Bauernfeind, Marc Scheffler, Valentyn Laguta, D. Kurt Gaskill, and Petr Neugebauer

Weak localization and antilocalization are characteristic signatures of coherent electronic transport in 2D systems. Here, employing contactless microwave magnetoconductivity experiments with frequencies much larger than the electron decoherence rate, the authors report transitions between weak localization and weak antilocalization in graphene induced by the microwave frequency. These transitions fall outside the predictions of established models, suggesting the existence of novel mechanisms affecting electron coherence at high microwave frequencies.


[Phys. Rev. B 113, 125408] Published Wed Mar 04, 2026
item.dc:title: Microwave-induced transitions between weak localization and weak antilocalization in graphene

item.title: Gauge theory and mixed state criticality
item.description:
Author(s): Takamasa Ando, Shinsei Ryu, and Masataka Watanabe

The authors develop here a systematic framework for constructing spontaneous symmetry-breaking (SSB) phases of strong symmetries, a concept specific to mixed quantum states. Starting from the ground-state phase diagram of lattice gauge theory models, the approach yields various mixed-state topological phases and explicit models for the critical points between them, including cases with gapless symmetry-protected topological order. They further clarify that lattice gauge theory ground states can be viewed as purifications of the corresponding mixed SSB states.


[Phys. Rev. B 113, 115106] Published Tue Mar 03, 2026
item.content:encoded:
Author(s): Takamasa Ando, Shinsei Ryu, and Masataka Watanabe

The authors develop here a systematic framework for constructing spontaneous symmetry-breaking (SSB) phases of strong symmetries, a concept specific to mixed quantum states. They further clarify that lattice gauge theory ground states can be viewed as purifications of the corresponding mixed SSB states.

item.title: Topological edge states in curved zigzag superlattices in nonlinear semiconductor microcavity exciton polaritons
item.description:
Author(s): Jing Wang, Tobias Schneider, Wei Hu, Stefan Schumacher, and Xuekai Ma

Topological edge states are highly sought-after in zigzag chains. Here, the authors introduce a zigzag superlattice composed of two sublattices that supports multiple topological edge states. In this configuration, intercell and intracell couplings are imbalanced through eigenstate tunneling or the deformation of higher-order modes. Arranging the superlattices into an arc structure encourages specific bulk states to become more localized at the edge. A similar state transformation can be achieved via repulsive nonlinearity, which also facilitates the coexistence of distinct edge states.


[Phys. Rev. B 113, 115302] Published Tue Mar 03, 2026
item.content:encoded:

A similar state transformation can be achieved via repulsive nonlinearity, which also facilitates the coexistence of distinct edge states.

item.title: Strain-induced gyrotropic effects in ferroelectric $\mathrm{BaTi}{\mathrm{S}}_{3}$
item.description:
Author(s): Wei Luo, Asier Zabalo, Guodong Ren, Gwan-Yeong Jung, Massimiliano Stengel, Rohan Mishra, Jayakanth Ravichandran, and Laurent Bellaiche

Here, the authors use first-principles calculations to uncover strain-engineered gyrotropic effects in ferroelectric BaTiS3. They predict a tensile strain induced transition to a chiral P63 phase enabling electric field switchable optical rotation, and a compressive strain driven insulator-to-polar Weyl semimetal transition that activates a nonlinear anomalous Hall effect with sign reversal. These results establish strain as a powerful route to control optical and transport responses in a single material.


[Phys. Rev. B 113, L100101] Published Tue Mar 03, 2026
item.content:encoded:
Author(s): Wei Luo, Asier Zabalo, Guodong Ren, Gwan-Yeong Jung, Massimiliano Stengel, Rohan Mishra, Jayakanth Ravichandran, and Laurent Bellaiche

They predict a tensile strain induced transition to a chiral P63 phase enabling electric field switchable optical rotation, and a compressive strain driven insulator-to-polar Weyl semimetal transition that activates a nonlinear anomalous Hall effect with sign reversal. These results establish strain as a powerful route to control optical and transport responses in a single material.


[Phys. Rev. B 113, L100101] Published Tue Mar 03, 2026

item.title: Diamagnetic Meissner response of odd-frequency superconducting pairing from quantum geometry
item.description:
Author(s): Ankita Bhattacharya and Annica M. Black-Schaffer

Odd-frequency superconductivity is ubiquitous in multiband systems but is commonly believed to yield a paramagnetic Meissner response and thus be unstable. The authors show here that this paradigm breaks down once quantum geometry is considered. For general two-band superconductors, the authors establish that the geometric interband contribution to the Meissner response from odd-frequency pairing is in fact diamagnetic, thus stabilizing odd-frequency superconductivity. In flat-band systems this geometric contribution can even dominate the Meissner response, which identifies flat-band materials as promising platforms for robust odd-frequency superconductivity.


[Phys. Rev. B 113, 094501] Published Mon Mar 02, 2026
item.content:encoded:

[Phys. Rev. B 113, 094501] Published Mon Mar 02, 2026

item.title: Symmetry-driven transitions between flat bands and Dirac cones in bilayer kagome lattices
item.description:
Author(s): Taylan Gorkan, Dogukan Hazar Ozbey, Cem Sevik, Milorad V. Milošević, and Engin Durgun

Flat bands (FBs) and Dirac cones represent two distinctive features of topological electronic systems; however, a unified mechanism enabling transitions between them has remained elusive to date. Here, we demonstrate a symmetry-governed and tunable transition from flat bands to Dirac cones in AB-sta…

item.description:
Author(s): Xudong Zhang, Zhaoyu Sun, and Bin Guo

The non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE) fundamentally invalidates the conventional bulk-boundary correspondence (BBC), leading topological diagnostics into a crisis. item.title: Resonating valence bond pairing energy in graphene by quantum Monte Carlo
item.description:
Author(s): S. Azadi, A. Principi, T. D. Kühne, and M. S. Bahramy

Whether short-range electron pairing can be stabilized in graphene remains a central question because pristine graphene is a semimetal with vanishing density of states at the Dirac point. We show that geometry alone can enhance short-range pairing correlations in confined graphene. Using real-space …

item.title: Statistical study of atomic, electronic, and mechanical properties of amorphous graphene
item.description:
Author(s): Jun Chai, Mingping He, Hangjia Shen, Pan Liu, Jia Zhang, and Yi-Yang Sun

Amorphous graphene (a-G), the disordered counterpart of crystalline graphene (c-G), holds promise for flexible electronics, catalysis, and energy storage, yet its atomic-scale structure and property origins remain elusive. Here, we introduce a helium potential-wall method within ab initio molecular …
